cards.back: h(t) = (B/π)sinc(B(t−td)/π). Sinc centred at td, extends to ±∞.
h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0. Sinc tails extend to −∞. System responds before input arrives.
P_W(ω) ↔ (W/2π)sinc(Wt/2π). Rectangle in freq → sinc in time.
sinc(x) = sin(πx)/(πx), with sinc(0) = 1. Zeros at all non-zero integers. Decays as 1/x.
No. Sinc tails decay as 1/t but never reach zero. h(t) is always non-zero for some t < 0.

cards.front: What does causality require for h(t)?
cards.back: h(t) = 0 for ALL t < 0. Output depends only on present and past inputs.
Yes. All four (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) have rectangular magnitude → sinc-type h(t) → non-causal.
All practical filters must have gradual transitions (no sharp cutoff). Leads to transition regions.

cards.front: Time-shift property for ideal LPF derivation?
cards.back: P_{2B}(ω)·e^{−jωtd} ↔ (B/π)sinc(B(t−td)/π). Multiplication by e^{−jωtd} → shift by td in time.

cards.front: Why does sharp frequency cutoff → long time response?
cards.back: Uncertainty principle: narrow in one domain → wide in the other. Rect (sharp) in freq → sinc (infinite) in time.
1/t — algebraic decay, never reaches zero. Much slower than exponential decay.

cards.front: Physical impossibility of h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0?
cards.back: The system would need to 'predict' the input before it arrives. Violates causality — no physical system can do this.
